title: Strait of Hormuz shipping traffic plunges over 90 percent amid Iran-US conflict
summary: British Royal Navy reports vessel transits through the Strait of Hormuz dropped from 130 per day to fewer than 10, with 41 security incidents recorded and 850+ vessels stranded in Gulf waters.
contents:
## Strait of Hormuz Shipping Traffic Collapse

### Dramatic Decline
The British Royal Navy, citing monitoring results from UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO), confirmed that shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has dropped from approximately 130 vessels per day before the conflict to fewer than 10 — a decline exceeding 90 percent.

### Security Incidents
Between March 1 and April 27, 2026, a total of 41 security incidents occurred in the Strait of Hormuz and surrounding waters. Reports of incidents "went through the roof" following the February 28 launch of US-Israeli military operations against Iran.

### Humanitarian Impact
Approximately 850 to 870 large merchant vessels — including oil tankers, LNG carriers, and container ships — now sit stranded in Gulf waters. Around 20,000 seafarers remain trapped in the region, with crew changes severely disrupted. Reduced supply deliveries and prolonged stranding threaten the mental health of seafarers.

### Global Consequences
The Strait of Hormuz normally carries approximately one-fifth of global oil and natural gas trade. The near-total shutdown has driven Brent crude prices above $110 per barrel, representing a 50+ percent increase since the conflict began. The disruption has triggered oil price relief programs in South Korea and fuel surcharge increases across Asian airlines.
